core_takeaway: No first-position advantage is evident. Agent_1 opened with its desired Apple/Pencil bundle, but the decisive consensus came from an efficient allocation that gave every agent its full-value top bundle, leaving the first and last positions tied.
why_or_counterexample: The counterexample is that the last speaker, Agent_3, proposed the clean allocation in public discussion, then all formal proposals converged on the same item sets and all agents accepted. Agent_1 did not beat Agent_3; both received 100 utility because their top-valued items did not conflict.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 set an early anchor around Apple and Pencil, but Agent_3's last-position discussion turn supplied the complete one-round allocation that satisfied all agents' top priorities. Agent_1 and Agent_2 then publicly aligned with it.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking is available for all three agents. Each agent's reasoning converged on the same Pareto-aligned allocation and treated possible concessions as restricted to zero-value or non-priority items.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: All three formal proposals used the same substantive allocation: Agent_1 received Apple and Pencil, Agent_2 received Jewel and Quill, and Agent_3 received Stone. Item order differed for Agent_2 but utility did not.
phase_notes.private_voting: All nine private votes accepted. The stated voting reasons emphasized maximum utility or top-item preservation for each voter, not deference to the first proposal.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection entries are present in the transcript.

mechanisms.theme: Orthogonal valuations made position less important
mechanisms.explanation: The agents' high-value targets were non-overlapping: Agent_1 valued Apple and Pencil, Agent_2 valued Jewel and Quill, and Agent_3 valued only Stone. Because the efficient allocation could satisfy everyone simultaneously, earlier speaking order did not create a payoff edge.

mechanisms.theme: The last-position speaker articulated the consensus allocation
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_3, speaking last in the initial discussion cycle, gave the complete allocation that later became the consensus. This weakens a first-position advantage interpretation because the last speaker's proposal preserved Agent_1's bundle while also securing Agent_3's own top item.

evidence.quote: Proposed one-round complete allocation (my preferred path):
- Agent_1 gets Apple and Pencil, its top two items.
- Agent_2 gets Quill and Jewel, its top two items.

mechanisms.explanation: The first proposal was not uniquely favored by its position. Each agent submitted the same substantive allocation, so the voting stage compared equivalent outcomes rather than an early mover's demand against later alternatives.

mechanisms.theme: Voting accepted maximum utility for all agents
mechanisms.explanation: The private votes show acceptance because the allocation maximized each voter's own utility, including the last-position agent. This is equality through mutual top-item satisfaction, not an earlier-position win.

evidence.quote: this is my best possible outcome in Round 1
maximizes my utility (Stone = 100) and avoids deadlock
